title: Vervain infusion for nervous tension
subtitle: A nerve-calming herb infusion for over-driven, tense states
excerpt: An infusion of dried vervain herb, steeped covered and drunk warm. A nerve-calming herb traditionally used for the over-stretched, over-busy reader who cannot stop and wind down.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, vervain entry: the nerve-calming herb, muscle-relaxing, and liver tradition. Nicholas Culpeper (the 17th-century herbalist), The English Physician, vervain entry: the herb's use for the exhausted and overwrought. King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), Verbena officinalis, the nerve-calming herb tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dried vervain stores 12 months in a sealed tin, cool and dark. Prepare each cup fresh.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that calms or tones the nervous system. Vervain is a distinctive nervine: traditionally indicated for the 'overdrive' state of tension rather than for simple anxiety or sleeplessness.

content.text: Vervain (Verbena officinalis) grows on roadsides across Britain and Europe. In the western herbal tradition it occupies a specific
niche. It is the herb for the reader who has pushed too hard. It is also used for exhaustion and nervous debility. The taste is bitter; this also helps stimulate digestion.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Pregnancy. Do not use vervain in pregnancy. Vervain has well-documented uterine-stimulating (uterine-stimulating) properties and is avoided throughout pregnancy.

Vervain has no significant drug interactions documented at standard infusion doses.

Burnout. Vervain is for mild tension and over-busyness, not for clinical burnout or depression. Tension that affects daily life or comes with mood disturbance needs medical assessment.

items.prepNote: dried Verbena officinalis aerial parts; about 1 teaspoon
optional; the bitterness is part of the preparation, but honey takes the edge off

content.text: Place the dried vervain in a lidded teapot. Pour over the just-boiled water. Put the lid on and steep covered for 10 minutes. The infusion will be pale green-gold and pleasantly bitter. Strain into a cup. Drink warm, ideally while sitting quietly, not at a desk.

One cup (250 ml, containing 2 g dried herb) up to three times a day. Traditionally taken at the end of the working day and again before bed. the The standard dose range is 2-4 g dried herb per dose. Take for up to 4 weeks continuously. Paediatric use: not for children under twelve without specialist herbalist advice.
Prepare each cup fresh. Dried vervain stores 12 months in a sealed tin, cool and dark.
When not to use this remedy
In pregnancy: do not use. Tension with chest pain, palpitations, or breathlessness needs medical assessment.
